skill.name: git-operation
skill.description: Guides agents through basic Git commit preparation, including English commit messages, formatting, and tests.
skill.summary: Prepare Git commits with consistent messages, formatting, and test checks.
skill.author: 5t111111

security_audit.summary: Manual review dismissed the weak cryptographic algorithm findings as false positives and found no prompt injection text. The skill is safe to publish with a medium warning because it directs agents to run repository-local `mise` commands before committing.

medium_findings.title: Repository Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run `mise run fmt` and `mise run test` before committing. This is legitimate for a Git workflow, but these commands execute repository-defined tasks and may run arbitrary local commands in untrusted projects.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present and relevant to the skill purpose. Risk depends on the repository mise configuration, so this is a contextual command-execution concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at lines 3, 8, and 16. Those lines contain prose about a Git guide and commit message formatting; no evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms or cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows natural-language Git instructions only. There are no crypto APIs, algorithm names, keys, hashes, or encryption logic in the file.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Execution Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static scanner classified Markdown code spans as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The backticks only format command names in documentation, although the documented commands remain an external-command risk when followed by an agent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited backticks appear in a Markdown bullet list, not executable Ruby code. The file is documentation-only, but the instructions can still cause command execution during use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to run `mise run fmt` and `mise run test` before committing. This is legitimate for a Git workflow, but these commands execute repository-defined tasks and may run arbitrary local commands in untrusted projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicitly present and relevant to the skill purpose. Risk depends on the repository mise configuration, so this is a contextual command-execution concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Inconsistent commit habits can lead to unclear history and unverified changes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through formatting, testing, and clear English commit messages.
actual_capabilities: Guides commit message writing in English.
Defines when to add detailed bullet points after a blank line.
Directs agents to run formatting before committing.
Directs agents to run tests before committing code changes.
Supports basic Git commit preparation workflows.
limitations: Does not define branch, merge, rebase, or release workflows.
Does not validate commit message length or enforce a specific convention.
Assumes the project has usable mise formatting and test tasks.
Does not inspect test results or fix failures by itself.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Clean Commit
use_cases.description: Run formatting and tests, then create a concise English commit message for the current changes.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Commit Habits
use_cases.description: Give AI assistants a simple checklist for commit message structure and pre-commit checks.

use_cases.title: Support Repository Maintenance
use_cases.description: Apply a repeatable commit preparation flow before submitting maintenance updates.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare My Commit
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current changes, run the required formatting and tests, and suggest a concise English commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize these changes in an English Git commit message. Use one concise first line and add bullets only if details are needed.

prompt_templates.title: Check Before Committing
prompt_templates.prompt: Before committing, verify formatting with mise run fmt and tests with mise run test. Report any failures clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Ready Commit Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the staged and unstaged changes, identify what should be committed together, run the required checks, and propose final commit messages.

output_examples.input: Prepare a commit for a small bug fix.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs formatting and tests, reports the results, and proposes a short English commit message.

output_examples.input: Create a commit message for a larger refactor.
output: A concise first line that summarizes the refactor.
Optional bullet points after a blank line when extra context is useful.
A note that formatting and tests should pass before committing.

output_examples.input: Check my repository before I commit.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the expected mise formatting and test commands, then summarizes whether the working tree is ready.

best_practices: Review repository task definitions before allowing formatting or test commands in untrusted projects.
Keep the first commit message line short, specific, and written in English.
Run formatting and tests before creating a commit that changes code.
anti_patterns: Do not run repository-defined commands in projects you do not trust.
Do not write vague commit messages such as update files or fix stuff.
Do not commit code changes after failing tests without documenting the reason.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants prepare Git commits with clear English messages, formatting checks, and test checks.

faq.question: Does it create commits automatically?
faq.answer: The skill gives commit preparation guidance. Actual commit creation depends on the assistant and user permissions.

faq.question: Why does it use mise commands?
faq.answer: It expects projects to define formatting and test tasks through mise, then runs those tasks before committing.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The mise tasks are repository-defined and may execute arbitrary local commands.

faq.question: Does it support Conventional Commits?
faq.answer: Not explicitly. It requires English messages and concise summaries, but does not enforce a specific convention.

faq.question: Can it fix failing tests?
faq.answer: No. It instructs the assistant to run tests and report failures, but fixing failures requires separate work.
